title: Business Intelligence Fundamentals
description: Core BI concepts: data, analytics, reporting, and decision support.

sections.title: Introduction to Business Intelligence
sections.lesson:
Introduction to Business Intelligence

Business Intelligence (BI) is a key concept in modern information systems. It explains how organizations collect, analyze, and use data to make informed decisions. This module introduces the foundations of BI and shows how it affects both organizations and daily digital experiences.

What is Business Intelligence?

Business Intelligence (BI) refers to the processes, technologies, and tools used to collect, process, analyze, and present data in order to support better decision-making.

BI transforms raw data into meaningful information through reports, dashboards, and visualizations.

Key idea: BI turns data into insights that help organizations decide what actions to take.

Evolution of Business Intelligence

Business Intelligence has evolved over time as technology improved:

  • Manual Reporting: Paper-based and spreadsheet reports created manually.
  • Management Information Systems (MIS): Automated, predefined reports.
  • Data Warehousing : Historical data analysis and multidimensional reporting.
  • Modern BI: Real-time dashboards, self-service analytics, and cloud-based systems.
Key idea: Modern BI provides faster, more flexible, and more visual insights.

Importance of Business Intelligence

Organizations use BI to:

  • Make data-driven decisions
  • Monitor performance using KPIs (Key Performance Indicators)
  • Identify trends and opportunities
  • Improve efficiency and reduce costs
  • Support long-term strategic planning
Key idea: BI reduces guesswork and improves decision accuracy.

Business Intelligence in Daily Life

BI is not limited to companies it is present in everyday digital platforms.

a. YouTube
  • Analyzes watch time, likes, and comments
  • Identifies trending and engaging videos
  • Supports content recommendations
b. Social Media
  • Measures post engagement
  • Tracks audience growth
  • Determines best posting times
c. Online Advertising
  • Monitors impressions and clicks
  • Calculates conversion rates and ROI
  • Optimizes ad placement and budget
Key idea: The content and ads you see online are influenced by BI analysis.

Types of Business Decisions Supported by BI

BI supports different levels of decision-making:

Decision Type Description Example
Operational Day-to-day decisions Daily inventory restocking
Tactical Medium-term decisions Adjusting marketing campaigns
Strategic Long-term decisions Entering a new market

Quick Activity: Identify the Decision Type

Classify each situation as Operational, Tactical, or Strategic:

  1. Deciding how many support staff are needed today
  2. Choosing which ad campaign to increase budget for
  3. Planning to invest in a new digital platform
Tip: Short-term = Operational, Medium-term = Tactical, Long-term = Strategic

sections.title: BI Framework
sections.lesson:
Business Intelligence Fundamentals

Business Intelligence (BI) evolved from earlier decision support systems and enterprise information systems. Its primary goal is to transform data into insights that support faster, smarter, and more informed business decisions.

Evolution of Business Intelligence

Decision support concepts were implemented gradually under various names. As enterprise-wide systems matured, managers gained access to user-friendly reports that supported quick decision making. These systems were initially known as Executive Information Systems (EIS).

Over time, EIS platforms added visualization tools, alerts, and performance measurement features. By 2006, these capabilities were collectively recognized under the term Business Intelligence (BI).

Key idea: BI is the modern evolution of DSS and EIS technologies.

Definition of Business Intelligence

Business Intelligence (BI) is an umbrella term that includes architectures, tools, databases, analytical applications, and methodologies used to analyze business data.

  • Provides interactive access to data
  • Supports real-time or near real-time analysis
  • Enables data manipulation and exploration
  • Helps transform data into decisions and actions
Key idea: BI turns data into information, decisions, and actions.

Architecture of BI

A typical BI system is composed of four major components:

  • Data Warehouse (DW): Stores integrated and historical data
  • Business Analytics: Tools for querying, reporting, and data mining
  • Business Performance Management (BPM): Monitors and evaluates performance
  • User Interface: Dashboards and visualization tools
Key idea: BI architecture separates data storage, analysis, and presentation.

Origins and Drivers of BI

Organizations invest in BI to better understand their data and improve decision making. Regulatory requirements, competitive pressure, and compressed business cycles have increased the demand for timely and accurate information.

  • Regulatory compliance (e.g., Sarbanes-Oxley Act)
  • Need for faster and better decisions
  • Increased accountability and transparency
Key idea: Managers need the right information at the right time.

Transaction Processing vs Analytic Processing

BI systems are designed for analysis, not for handling daily transactions. Online Transaction Processing (OLTP) systems manage routine business operations such as sales, deposits, and inventory updates.

In contrast, Online Analytical Processing (OLAP) systems use data warehouses to analyze historical and current data for decision support.

Aspect OLTP OLAP
Purpose Daily operations Analysis and decision support
Data Current, detailed Historical, summarized
Users Clerks, operators Managers, analysts

Planning and Alignment with Business Strategy

BI initiatives must align with organizational strategy. BI is not just a technical project—it is a business transformation tool that promotes data-driven decision making.

Many organizations establish a BI Competency Center to support best practices, collaboration, and alignment between business users and IT teams.

Key idea: Successful BI starts with strategy alignment.

Real-Time and On-Demand BI

Modern BI increasingly supports real-time or near real-time access to data. Technologies such as sensors, RFID, and event-driven systems enable organizations to monitor operations as they happen.

  • Real-time alerts and notifications
  • Automated responses to business events
  • Business Activity Management (BAM)
Key idea: Real-time BI enables agile and proactive decision making.

Developing or Acquiring BI Systems

Organizations can build, purchase, or lease BI solutions. Many vendors offer pre-built BI platforms that can be customized to business needs.

The choice depends on cost, flexibility, expertise, and long-term strategy.

Security, Privacy, and Integration

BI systems often contain sensitive and strategic data. Ensuring data security and protecting privacy are critical requirements.

BI solutions must also integrate with other systems such as ERP, CRM, and e-commerce platforms to deliver maximum value.

Key idea: Secure, integrated BI systems lead to better and safer insights.
Business Intelligence, Analytics, Data Science, and AI A Managerial Perspective (5th Edition)

sections.title: BI Data Concepts
sections.lesson:
Module 2: Data Fundamentals

Data is the foundation of Business Intelligence. Before data can be analyzed or visualized, it is important to understand what data is, how it differs from information and knowledge, and the different forms data can take in modern information systems.

1. Data, Information, and Knowledge

Data refers to raw facts and figures without context.
Information is processed data that has meaning.
Knowledge is the understanding gained from analyzing information.

Concept Description Example
Data Raw, unprocessed facts 100, 200, 300
Information Processed and meaningful data Daily sales = ₱300
Knowledge Insights used for decisions Sales are increasing
Key idea: BI focuses on converting data into information and knowledge.

2. Types of Data by Structure

Data can be classified based on how it is organized:

  • Structured Data: Organized in tables with rows and columns (e.g., databases).
  • Semi-Structured Data: Has structure but not in table form (e.g., JSON, XML).
  • Unstructured Data: No predefined structure (e.g., text, images, videos).
Key idea: BI systems must handle different data structures.

3. Types of Data by Business Use

  • Transactional Data: Records daily operations (sales, payments).
  • Behavioral Data: Tracks user actions (clicks, views).
  • Demographic Data: Describes users (age, location).

These data types are commonly used together in BI analysis.

4. Data Sources

BI systems collect data from multiple sources:

  • Internal Sources: Sales systems, HR systems, inventory databases
  • External Sources: Social media, market data, third-party providers
Key idea: Combining internal and external data improves insights.

5. Data Quality Issues

Good BI depends on high-quality data. Common data quality problems include:

  • Missing values
  • Duplicate records
  • Incorrect or outdated data
  • Inconsistent formats
Key idea: Poor data quality leads to poor decisions.

6. Data Fundamentals in Daily Life

Everyday platforms use different data types:

  • YouTube uses behavioral data (watch time)
  • Social media uses engagement data
  • Online stores use transactional data

7. Quick Activity: Identify the Data Type

Identify the type of data used in each

sections.title: OLTP vs OLAP
sections.lesson:
Database Systems

Database systems are designed to serve different purposes. Some focus on handling daily transactions efficiently, while others focus on analyzing large volumes of data to support decision-making. These two approaches are known as OLTP and OLAP.

What is OLTP?

Online Transaction Processing (OLTP) systems are designed to manage day-to-day operations of an organization. They handle a large number of short, fast transactions such as inserting, updating, or deleting records.

  • Supports many concurrent users
  • Processes frequent transactions
  • Works with current, highly detailed data
  • Emphasizes speed and accuracy

Example systems:

  • Banking systems (ATM withdrawals, deposits)
  • Online shopping systems (orders, payments)
  • Library management systems (borrowing and returning books)
  • Student information systems (enrollment, grades)

UPDATE accounts
SET balance = balance - 1000
WHERE account_id = 123;

What is OLAP?

Online Analytical Processing (OLAP) systems are designed for data analysis and reporting. They help organizations analyze historical data to discover patterns, trends, and insights for better decision-making.

  • Used by managers and analysts
  • Handles large volumes of historical data
  • Executes complex queries
  • Primarily read-only operations

Example systems:

  • Sales data warehouses
  • Business Intelligence (BI) dashboards
  • Financial reporting systems
  • Academic performance analytics systems

SELECT year, SUM(total_sales)
FROM sales
GROUP BY year;

Key Differences Between OLTP and OLAP

Feature OLTP OLAP
Main Purpose Run daily operations Analyze data
Data Type Current, detailed data Historical, summarized data
Operations INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE SELECT, AGGREGATE
Query Complexity Simple and short Complex and long
Users Clerks, customers, staff Managers, analysts
Performance Focus Transaction speed Query depth and insight

OLTP and OLAP in Practice

In real-world systems, OLTP databases handle daily business transactions, while OLAP databases analyze accumulated data from these transactions. Together, they support both operational efficiency and strategic decision-making.

Business Intelligence, Analytics, Data Science, and AI A Managerial Perspective (5th Edition)

sections.title: BI Analytics
sections.lesson:
Business Analytics

Analytics is the process of examining data to discover patterns, trends, and insights that support effective decision-making. Organizations use analytics to understand past performance, predict future outcomes, and determine the best actions to take.

What is Analytics?

Analytics transforms raw data into meaningful information that helps answer key business questions such as what happened, what is likely to happen, and what should be done next. As analytics maturity increases, so does its value to decision-makers.

Descriptive Analytics

Descriptive analytics focuses on summarizing historical data to understand past performance. It answers the question: "What happened?"

  • Uses historical data
  • Provides reports and dashboards
  • Identifies trends and patterns

Example: Monthly enrollment reports showing the number of students per course.

Predictive Analytics

Predictive analytics uses historical and current data along with statistical models to forecast future outcomes. It answers the question: "What is likely to happen?"

  • Uses trend analysis and forecasting
  • Applies statistical and machine learning models
  • Estimates probabilities of future events

Example: Predicting student dropout risk based on attendance and grades.

Prescriptive Analytics

Prescriptive analytics recommends actions by analyzing data and predicted outcomes. It answers the question: "What should we do?"

  • Uses optimization and simulation
  • Builds on descriptive and predictive analytics
  • Provides decision recommendations

Example: Recommending additional course offerings to avoid enrollment decline.

Comparison of Analytics Types

Analytics Type Main Question Focus Output
Descriptive What happened? Past data Reports and dashboards
Predictive What will happen? Future trends Forecasts and probabilities
Prescriptive What should we do? Decision optimization Recommended actions

Analytics in Practice

In real-world systems, descriptive analytics provides insights into past performance, predictive analytics forecasts future outcomes, and prescriptive analytics guides decision-making. Together, they enable data-driven strategies and smarter planning.

Business Intelligence, Analytics, Data Science, and AI A Managerial Perspective (5th Edition)

sections.title: BI Reporting
sections.lesson:
Impute Widget

1. Handling Missing Data

Ensure dataset integrity by replacing gaps using averages, constant values, or model-based predictions (1-NN).

Impute Documentation
Similar: Missing Data Strategies
Preprocess Widget

2. Data Normalization

Standardize feature scales to prevent high-magnitude numbers (like revenue) from biasing distance-based algorithms.

Normalization Guide
Similar: Preprocessing Deep Dive
Rank Widget

3. Feature Selection

Use Information Gain or Chi-Square to identify which business attributes most impact your target KPI.

Ranking Documentation
Similar: Feature Subset Selection
Tree Viewer Widget

4. Decision Tree Model

Generate transparent "If-Then" rules that allow business managers to see the logic behind every prediction.

Tree Viewer Documentation
Similar: Visualizing Logic
Test & Score Widget

5. Model Comparison

Contrast multiple algorithms (Random Forest vs LogReg) using cross-validation to select the best performer.

Testing Documentation
Similar: Evaluation Metrics 101
Confusion Matrix Widget

6. Confusion Matrix

Analyze specific prediction errors to calculate the financial impact of False Positives vs False Negatives.

Matrix Documentation
Similar: Error Analysis Blog
Scatter Plot / Box Plot

7. Data Visualization

Create visual stories to spot trends. Use the 'Find Informative Projections' feature to automate insight discovery.

Visual Catalog
Similar: Advanced Viz Tricks
Select Data / Interactivity

8. Dashboard Design

Link widgets together so that clicking a point in one chart filters a table in another—perfect for live BI demos.

Interactive Design
Similar: Workflow Templates
Scoring Sheet / Explainers

9. Decision Making

Translate model scores into a final "Yes/No" decision sheet. Use 'Explain Model' to defend your strategy to stakeholders.

Explainable AI (XAI)
Similar: BI Strategy Theory
Workflow Integration

10. BI Workflow

Combine data ingestion, cleaning, modeling, and reporting into a single, automated, and repeatable system.

Workflow Library
Similar: YouTube: Beginner to Pro
© 2026 BI Team | Powered by Orange Data Mining

sections.title: Decision Support Systems
sections.lesson:
Importance of Decision Making

Decision making is one of the most important activities in organizations of all kinds. It determines the success or failure of organizations and directly impacts performance.

Why Decision Making is Difficult

  • Internal and external factors make decision making complex
  • Rewards for good decisions can be high
  • Losses from wrong decisions can also be very high
  • Many decisions are not simple and require different approaches
Note: It is essential to understand the nature of decision making before choosing the appropriate approach. For more details, see De Smet et al. (2017).

Modern Business Environment

Modern business is full of uncertainties and rapid changes. Decision makers must handle ever-increasing and changing data to make effective decisions.

This book focuses on technologies that help decision makers analyze data and support decision making processes.

Decision-Making Process

Introduction

Decision making used to be viewed as an art, relying mainly on managers’ experience, intuition, creativity, and judgment. However, modern research emphasizes that methodical, analytical decision making produces better outcomes than relying solely on interpersonal skills.

From Art to Science

  • Managers used trial and error
  • Decisions were based on intuition and experience
  • Decision styles varied from person to person

Today, decision making is viewed as analytical and systematic, requiring managers to use data-driven approaches. Persistence and analytical thinking are more valuable than charisma.

Role of Analytics in Decision Making

Modern businesses use analytics to:

  • Describe what is happening (reporting)
  • Predict what will happen (forecasting)
  • Decide what actions to take (decision support)

This process requires collecting large amounts of data and analyzing it using advanced tools and technologies.

Growth of Data

Data volume is rapidly increasing. The amount of data doubles every two years, and organizations must manage this growth efficiently.

Modern BI Tools

Managers rely on tools such as:

  • Data Warehousing
  • Data Mining
  • OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)
  • Dashboards
  • Cloud-based systems

Role of Information Systems

Modern management depends on high-speed networked systems (wired or wireless) with real-time data access, including mobile integration.

Most important managerial task: Making decisions.

Automation of Decision Making

Many decisions are now automated using AI, machine learning, and rule-based systems, reducing the need for human intervention in routine decisions.

Technologies for Data Analysis and Decision Support

Growth in Technology

Beyond improvements in hardware, software, and networks, several technology developments have supported the growth of decision support and analytics.

Group Communication and Collaboration

Many decisions are made by groups in different locations. Collaboration tools and smartphones enable easy communication, especially during events like the COVID-19 pandemic. This reduces travel costs and improves collaboration across supply chains.

Improved Data Management

Complex decisions often require data from different databases across organizations. Modern systems can store, search, transmit, and manage data quickly, securely, and efficiently, even when it includes text, audio, graphics, and video.

Managing Giant Data Warehouses and Big Data

Large data warehouses contain massive data volumes. Technologies such as parallel computing, Hadoop/Spark, and cloud-based systems help organize, search, and mine these data stores. Costs of storage and data mining are rapidly declining.

Analytical Support

More data and advanced analysis tools allow organizations to:

  • Evaluate many alternatives
  • Improve forecasts
  • Perform quick risk analysis
  • Collect expert views remotely
  • Run complex simulations and scenarios

Overcoming Cognitive Limits

According to Simon (1977), human minds have limited ability to process and store information. Computer systems help overcome these limits by quickly accessing and processing vast information.

Knowledge Management

Organizations have collected huge amounts of information from operations, customers, employees, and stakeholders. Knowledge management systems (KMS) help support decision making. Text analytics can generate value from these knowledge stores.

Anywhere, Anytime Support

Mobile technologies enable managers to access information anytime and anywhere. This has dramatically increased the speed of decision-making and changed expectations for both businesses and consumers.

Innovation and Artificial Intelligence

AI supports innovation in decision making by improving many steps of the decision process. AI combined with analytics creates synergy in making better decisions.

Chapter 1.2-1.3 Business Intelligence, Analytics, Data Science, and AI A Managerial Perspective (5th Edition)
